skill.name: llm-council
skill.description: Orchestrates a configurable council of CLI-based AI agents to create, compare, and merge implementation plans. It runs planner agents, anonymizes their Markdown plans, judges the results, and writes a final plan with run artifacts.
skill.summary: Run a multi-agent planning council that compares independent AI plans and produces one final implementation plan.
skill.author: am-will

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most Markdown backtick, weak-crypto, path traversal, and C2 keyword detections are false positives. The confirmed risks are high-impact: the skill launches external CLI agents, supports custom commands, uses a permission-bypass CLI flag, writes artifacts, and exposes local UI state. No evidence found of malicious credential theft or command-and-control intent, so this is high risk rather than blocked critical.

high_findings.title: User-Configurable Command Execution
high_findings.description: The skill supports custom planner and judge commands from task or config data, then launches them with subprocess.Popen. This is intended for extensibility, but a malicious or careless config can execute arbitrary local commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly builds subprocess arguments from configurable command and extra_args fields. This is a real execution path, although it appears to be an intended feature rather than hidden behavior.

high_findings.title: Agent CLI Launched With Permission Bypass Flag
high_findings.description: The Claude CLI command includes a permission bypass option while running model prompts. The command also disables tools, but the bypass flag is still a high-risk execution setting for a marketplace skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous permission flag is present in the command list used by the subprocess launcher. The surrounding CLI flags reduce some risk, but the pattern remains security-sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Repository Context Sent To External AI CLIs
medium_findings.description: Planner prompts instruct agents to read the codebase and the orchestrator passes task context to Codex, Claude, Gemini, OpenCode, or custom CLIs. This can disclose repository content to external model providers depending on user configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill purposefully invokes external AI command-line tools with task prompts and repo-reading instructions. This is legitimate for the workflow but creates clear data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Local UI State Readable Without Token
medium_findings.description: The UI requires a token for events and POST actions, but state endpoints return task brief, planner summaries, judge output, and final plan without token validation. On localhost this is lower risk, but it can expose sensitive planning content to local processes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The GET handlers for state do not call the token validator, while the state includes plan and task content. Binding to 127.0.0.1 limits exposure but does not eliminate local information disclosure.

medium_findings.title: Long-Lived Session Keepalive Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to keep the session open for up to 30 minutes and the UI can remain open while keepalive is enabled. This can consume resources and keep sensitive state available longer than expected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented and implemented, but it is not evidence of malicious persistence. The risk is operational and privacy-related rather than covert execution.

low_findings.title: Expected Filesystem Writes To Config And Run Artifacts
low_findings.description: The skill writes agent configuration, UI snapshots, planner outputs, judge output, and final plans. These writes are core functionality, but users should run it only in trusted workspaces and inspect output paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and confirmed, but they are transparent artifacts of the planning workflow. No evidence found of hidden destructive file operations.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick, Weak-Crypto, And Path Findings Mostly False Positive
low_findings.description: Many static hits come from Markdown inline code, JSON schema identifiers, Markdown template text, and fixed relative reference paths. No evidence found of Ruby execution, weak cryptographic operations, command-and-control code, or credential exfiltration in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show documentation, schema metadata, or fixed paths rather than dynamic Ruby, crypto, or traversal behavior. This does not dismiss the confirmed subprocess risks elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Limited To Config Location
low_findings.description: The environment access found by static analysis reads XDG_CONFIG_HOME to choose the agent configuration path. No evidence found that environment variables are collected or transmitted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads one standard configuration environment variable and falls back to the user config directory. There is no network call or credential handling connected to this access.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Configurable Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill supports custom planner and judge commands from task or config data, then launches them with subprocess.Popen. This is intended for extensibility, but a malicious or careless config can execute arbitrary local commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly builds subprocess arguments from configurable command and extra_args fields. This is a real execution path, although it appears to be an intended feature rather than hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent CLI Launched With Permission Bypass Flag
dangerous_patterns.description: The Claude CLI command includes a permission bypass option while running model prompts. The command also disables tools, but the bypass flag is still a high-risk execution setting for a marketplace skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous permission flag is present in the command list used by the subprocess launcher. The surrounding CLI flags reduce some risk, but the pattern remains security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.description: The UI requires a token for events and POST actions, but state endpoints return task brief, planner summaries, judge output, and final plan without token validation. On localhost this is lower risk, but it can expose sensitive planning content to local processes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The GET handlers for state do not call the token validator, while the state includes plan and task content. Binding to 127.0.0.1 limits exposure but does not eliminate local information disclosure.

content.value_statement: Complex coding changes often need more than one planning perspective. This skill runs several AI planner CLIs, compares their plans, and produces one consolidated implementation plan.
actual_capabilities: Configures planner and judge agents for Codex, Claude, Gemini, OpenCode, or custom CLIs.
Runs multiple planner agents and captures each Markdown plan as an artifact.
Anonymizes and randomizes valid plans before the judge evaluates them.
Produces judge output and a final Markdown implementation plan.
Provides a local UI for monitoring, saving, accepting, and refining final plans.
Stores run metadata, validation status, warnings, and plan files under a run directory.
limitations: Requires the selected AI CLIs to be installed and authenticated before use.
May send task and repository context to external model providers through those CLIs.
Plan quality depends on the configured models and the task brief quality.
Custom commands can execute local programs and should be used only with trusted configs.

use_cases.title: Compare Architecture Plans
use_cases.description: Generate independent implementation plans for a complex feature, then merge the strongest ideas into one plan.

use_cases.title: Review Risky Refactors
use_cases.description: Ask several models to identify edge cases, test needs, and rollback steps before a large refactor starts.

use_cases.title: Standardize Planning Artifacts
use_cases.description: Create consistent Markdown plans with validation, judge notes, and saved run history for team review.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a focused task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-council to plan this small code change: [describe change]. Ask me intake questions first, then run the council with default agents.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Refactor Approaches
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for a shared module refactor.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-council to compare refactor plans for [module]. Include constraints, affected paths, test expectations, and rollback requirements in the task brief.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-council to plan a migration from [current system] to [target system]. Require each planner to cover compatibility, data safety, deployment, monitoring, and rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Custom Council
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with selected models and judge.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-council with my configured agents to produce a final plan for [initiative]. Keep custom command use limited to the trusted config I provide.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe redesign of an authentication module with tests and rollback steps.
output_examples.output: A final Markdown plan that summarizes the chosen approach, implementation phases, edge cases, tests, deployment checks, and rollback actions.

output_examples.input: Compare options for replacing a background job runner in a production application.
output_examples.output: A judged plan that lists tradeoffs from multiple planner outputs and recommends one practical migration path.

output_examples.input: Refine an existing final plan after a reviewer adds new constraints.
output_examples.output: An updated final plan saved as a new artifact and synchronized in the local UI.

best_practices: Use a clear task brief with constraints, affected paths, success criteria, and required tests.
Review generated plans before implementation because planner and judge outputs are untrusted content.
Run custom commands only from trusted configuration files in repositories that can safely use external AI tools.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill on confidential repositories unless provider data policies allow the selected CLIs.
Do not paste secrets, credentials, or production tokens into task briefs or refinement requests.
Do not configure arbitrary custom commands from untrusted users or copied examples.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates planner outputs, judge notes, metadata, and one final Markdown implementation plan.

faq.question: Which AI tools can it orchestrate?
faq.answer: It supports Codex, Claude, Gemini, OpenCode, and custom command-line agents when configured.

faq.question: Does it modify application source code?
faq.answer: No. It writes planning artifacts and configuration files, but the plans may instruct later code changes.

faq.question: Can it expose private code?
faq.answer: Yes. Selected external CLIs may receive task and repository context, so users must verify provider policies first.

faq.question: Why are plans anonymized before judging?
faq.answer: Anonymization and random ordering reduce model and position bias during plan comparison.

faq.question: Should marketplace users run custom commands?
faq.answer: Only when the config is trusted. Custom commands are powerful and can execute local programs.
